comments.text: let me share a reduction to a nicer sounding problem, which was observed several months ago with Micha Christoph, Annika Heckel, and Raphael Steiner. imagine we instead sample $G_1,G_2\sim G(n,1/2)$ independently. let $X$ be the random variable for the smallest $k$ so that there exists a partition of $[n]$ into $k$ sets $I_1,\dots,I_k$ so that each $I_j$ is an independent set in either $G_1$ or $G_2$. then we couple $X$ so that it is always greater than $\zeta(G)$ for a random $G\sim G(n,1/2)$. the proof is a form of the "McDiarmid's coupling"-technique, which is a very slick trick I think more people should know! now, $\chi(G)$ concentrates inside an interval of length $n^{1/2+o(1)}$ around its expectation (by an old result of Noga). and a similar result should say $X$ should concentrate in an interval of length $n^{1/2+o(1)}$ around its mean too. thus it would suffice to compute $\mathbb{E}[X]$ to sufficient accuracy, or instead simply show that: $\mathbb{P}(\chi(G_1)-X>n^{1/2+\Omega(1)})>\Omega(1)$.
Do you have a conjecture what the behaviour of $\mathbb{E}(X)$ is? Could this approach prove the conjecture of Heckel that\[\chi(G)-\zeta(G)\asymp \frac{n}{(\log n)^3}\]with high probability?

yes I believe it should still imply this. maybe you'll get the wrong constant now. I'll try asking Annika later (we both currently are at Oberwolfach). EDIT: yes indeed, you should still get the same behaviour.
